coredata.dc:title: Au@Carbon quantum Dots-MXene nanocomposite as an electrochemical sensor for sensitive detection of nitrite
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Colloid and Interface Science
coredata.dc:creator: Feng, Xiwen
Han, Guangda
Cai, Jihai
Wang, Xiaoying
coredata.dc:description: A highly sensitive electrochemical sensor was developed through a one-pot green synthesis method for nitrite detection based on the electrochemical technique. Xylan-based carbon quantum dots (CQDs) were used as green in situ reducing agent to prepare CQDs capped gold nanoparticles (Au@CQDs). MXene of good electrical conductivity was used as the immobilized matrix to fabricate Au@CQDs-MXene nanocomposites with the advantages of good electrical conductivity and electrocatalysis. An electrochemical sensor for nitrite monitor was obtained by loading the Au@CQDs-MXene on a glassy carbon electrode. The sensor presents high sensitivity, good stability, wide linear range, and excellent selectivity due to the high catalytic activity of AuNPs and CQDs, the large specific surface area of MXene, and exceptional electrical conductivity of AuNPs and MXene. Under the optimal condition, the linear detection range of the sensor was from 1 μM to 3200 μM with a detection limit of 0.078 μM (S/N = 3), which was superior to most reported sensors using differential pulse voltammetry (DPV) method. Furthermore, this sensor was successfully applied to detect nitrite in tap water and salted vegetables with satisfactory recoveries. This modified electrocatalytic sensor shows a new pathway to fabricate nitrite detection sensor with feasibility for practical application.
coredata.dcterms:subject: One-pot green synthesis
Au@CQDs-MXene nanocomposites
Sensitive detection

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Journal of Colloid and Interface Science

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: The preparation of xylan carbon dots
Characterization of CQDs, MXene, Au@CQDs, Au@CQDs-MXene
Electrochemical behaviors of GCE and modified GCEs

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) the UV–Vis spectra of CQDs, MXene, Au@CQDs, Au@CQDs-MXene; (b) XRD patterns of MAX, MXene, CQDs, CQDs-MXene and Au@CQDs-MXene nanocomposite; (c) FT-IR spectra of MAX, MXene, Au@CQDs, CQDs-MXene, Au@CQDs-MXene; (d) XPS spectrums of the Au@CQDs-MXene; (e) Au4f; (f) Ti2p.
SEM images of (a) MXene; (b) Au@CQDs; (c) Au@CQDs-MXene; TEM images of (d) MXene; (e) Au@CQDs, the inset is HRTEM image of AuNPs; (f) Au@CQDs-MXene.
(a) TEM images of Au@CQDs-MXene; the corresponding EDS elemental mapping of (b) four elements, (c) Ti, (d) N, (e) O and (f) Au.

(a) CV results of modified electrodes for nitrite detection in 0.1 M PBS (pH 7.0) containing 800 μM NO2 – with scan rate 0.1 V s−1; (b) Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy results of modified electrodes for nitrite (pH 7.0); (c) CV curves of 800 μM nitrite with Au@CQDs-MXene/GCE in 0.1 M PBS (pH 7.0) at different scan rates and the calibration plot of peak current vs. square root of scan rates; (d) CV curves of 800 μM nitrite with Au@CQDs-MXene/GCE in different pH values and the relationship between the peak current and pH.

(a) DPV response of the Au@CQDs-MXene modified GCE in 0.1 M PBS (pH 7.0) with different concentrations of nitrite (0 μM-3.2 mM) at the applied potential of 100 mV and the inset shows the enlarged view of the response of nitrite from 0 to 200 μM; (b) Corresponding calibration curves of the DPV response current vs. the concentration of nitrite; (c) The current–time curve of Au@CQDs-MXene/GCE after adding 100 μM NO2 – and various common salt solutions as interference in 0.1 M PBS (pH 7.0); (d) CV curves of 100 μM nitrite with the same one GCE modified with Au@CQDs-MXene after different time (from a to h: 0, 2,4,6,8,10,12,14 days).
Fabrication of Au@CQDs-MXene/GCE for nitrite detection.
Determination of nitrite in tap water.
Determination of nitrite in salted vegetables.
Detecting performances of different modified electrodes for nitrite detection.

head.ce:title: Au@Carbon quantum Dots-MXene nanocomposite as an electrochemical sensor for sensitive detection of nitrite

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Pulp & Paper Engineering, South China University of Technology, Wushan Road, Tianhe District, Guangzhou 510640, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Pulp & Paper Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State Key Laboratory of Pulp & Paper Engineering, South China University of Technology, Wushan Road, Tianhe District, Guangzhou 510640, China

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These two authors contributed equally to this work

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A highly sensitive electrochemical sensor was developed through a one-pot green synthesis method for nitrite detection based on the electrochemical technique. Xylan-based carbon quantum dots (CQDs) were used as green in situ reducing agent to prepare CQDs capped gold nanoparticles (Au@CQDs). MXene of good electrical conductivity was used as the immobilized matrix to fabricate Au@CQDs-MXene nanocomposites with the advantages of good electrical conductivity and electrocatalysis. An electrochemical sensor for nitrite monitor was obtained by loading the Au@CQDs-MXene on a glassy carbon electrode. The sensor presents high sensitivity, good stability, wide linear range, and excellent selectivity due to the high catalytic activity of AuNPs and CQDs, the large specific surface area of MXene, and exceptional electrical conductivity of AuNPs and MXene. Under the optimal condition, the linear detection range of the sensor was from 1 μM to 3200 μM with a detection limit of 0.078 μM (S/N = 3), which was superior to most reported sensors using differential pulse voltammetry (DPV) method. Furthermore, this sensor was successfully applied to detect nitrite in tap water and salted vegetables with satisfactory recoveries. This modified electrocatalytic sensor shows a new pathway to fabricate nitrite detection sensor with feasibility for practical application.

ce:section.ce:para: Nitrite is intensively used in food and chemical industries as food preservatives, color formers, rust inhibitors and so on. The leakage or pollution discharge leads to the enrichment of nitrite in water sources [1,2]. Noteworthily, listed as a carcinogen by the World Health Organization, nitrite is a virulent inorganic contaminant that irreversibly damages human health. Excessive or long-term intake will cause a type of cancer called methemoglobinemia [3]. Therefore, the determination of nitrite has attached extensive attention.

Numerous analytical techniques have been proposed for the determination of nitrite, such as colorimetry, spectrophotometry, gas chromatography-mass spectrometry (GC–MS) [1], liquid chromatography-mass spectrometry (LC-MS) [4], and electrochemistry [5]. Among these techniques, electrochemical methods stick out due to their outstanding advantages like low cost, high precision, fast response, and high portability [1]. In the process of electrochemical detection, nitrite is oxidized to nitrate on the surface of the electrode [6,7]. Meanwhile, the oxidation current is recorded by the electrochemical workstation.

Glassy carbon (GC) is commonly used as basic electrode material in electrochemical sensors for nitrite detection because nitrite has electroactivity on GC [8]. Many metal nanoparticles, carbon-based materials and conducting polymers with great physical and chemical characteristics are utilized to modify the glassy carbon electrode to promote its catalytic and electron capture/transportation properties [9]. For example, metal nanoparticles include gold nanoparticles (AuNPs) [10], palladium nanocubes (PdNCs) [11], platinum nanoparticles (PtNPs) [12], carbon-based nanomaterials such as carbon quantum dots (CQDs) [13], graphene [14], carbon nanotubes (CNTs) [15], conducting polymers polyaniline (PANI) [16] and MXene [17] are commonly used to form innovative sensors. Among these materials, AuNPs are extensively utilized owing to their distinct advantages such as outstanding catalysis activity and high surface-area-to-volume ratio [18]. Nevertheless, toxic reagents such as tannic acid [18], ethylene glycol [19], and NaBH4 [20] are always used in the chemical reduction of AuNPs. However, under the threat of hazardous and ecologically unfriendly chemicals which destroyed the global environment, green chemistry principles were proposed by chemistry researchers. To avoid using those toxic reagents, CQDs were utilized to prepare AuNPs. Furthermore, due to the hydroxyl groups, CQDs showed good reducing ability as green reducing agents [21]. Therefore, CQDs can be not only used as green reducing agents but also used as in-suit growing sites for the synthesis of AuNPs [22]. Nevertheless, Au@CQDs is nanophase material with the shortcoming of easy agglomeration, which needs attachment sites to prevent them from agglomerating. MXene is a sort of neoteric two-dimensional inorganic compound with a unique layered structure [23,24]. The layered structure provides a large specific surface area and an abundance of functional groups on the surface [25], which may act as an immobilized matrix to disperse Au@CQDs [26]. Moreover, its high electronic conductivity may help to construct a highly sensitive modified electrode of Au@CQDs-MXene nanomaterial. At the same time, Au@CQDs can also prevent the agglomeration of MXene. Au@CQDs with excellent conductivity surmount the negative effect of too many oxygen-containing functional groups on MXene. So, the oxidation reaction of nitrite is easier to happen with the help of Au@CQDs-MXene. Furthermore, this nanocomposite was synthesized based on a one-pot method using as-prepared CQDs and MXene, which is easy and convenient to operate.

In this paper, a highly sensitive electrochemical sensor based on Au@CQDs-MXene nanocomposite for nitrite detection was developed (Scheme 1 ). CQDs made from xylan were used as green in situ reducing agents of AuNPs, while Au@CQDs were attached to the surface of MXene. In this way, Au@CQDs-MXene nanocomposite was obtained. There is no chemical reducing agent used in the green one-pot synthesis of Au@CQDs-MXene nanocomposite. These three components in Au@CQDs-MXene nanocomposite have good electrical conductivity and electrocatalysis, and Au shows the greatest improvement in electrocatalysis of the modified GCE. MXene is also significant factor to enhance the sensitivity of the modified electrode in terms of their good conductivity. As a result, the Au@CQDs-MXene/GCE showed high sensitivity and good selectivity to nitrite detection. Under optimal condition, a good linear relationship between differential pulse voltammetry (DPV) peak current and nitrite concentration was obtained and divided into two linear ranges according to the nitrite concentration. The first detection scope was from 1 μM to 500 μM, while the other detection scope was from 500 μM to 3200 μM. Moreover, the detection limit to nitrite concentration was estimated to be only 0.078 μM (S/N = 3). Furthermore, this sensor was successfully applied to detect nitrite in tap water and salted vegetables.

Xylan extracted from bagasse was provided by Yuanye Bio-Technology Co., Ltd. (Shanghai China). Chloroauric acid (HAuCl4, 99%), sodium nitrite (NaNO2, 99%), and sodium hydroxide (NaOH, 99%) were gained from Macklin Biochemical Co., Ltd. (Shanghai China). Other chemicals such as NaCl, Na2SO4, KNO3, MgCl2, CaCl2, NaAc, CuCl2 are all common products on the market. The salted vegetables (Sichuan Minfuji Food Co., Ltd.) were brought in the locate market (Guangzhou, China). All of the reagents used in the experiments were of analytical grade without further treatment.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The preparation of xylan carbon dots
ce:section.ce:para: 1 g of xylan was dissolved in 20 mL of NaOH/urea aqueous solution (8%/12%, w/w). Then the mixed solution was poured into a 25 mL stainless steel Teflon-liner autoclave and reacted at 240 °C for 24 h. Then the mixed solution was centrifugated at 14000 rpm for 10 min to remove the precipitate. Dialyzing by a dialysis bag of 500 Da molecular weight cut off (MWCO) for a week, CQDs were obtained and then stored at room temperature in a dark place.

1.5 g of Ti3AlC2 and 2 g of LiF were slowly added to 20 mL of 12 mol/L HCl and the mixture was magnetically stirred at 35 °C for 24 h. The obtained solution was centrifuged and washed with deionized water until the pH was greater than 6. Then the precipitate obtained by centrifugation was dispersed in the ultrapure water and sonicated for 15 min. Finally, the liquid was centrifuged again at the speed of 3500 rpm for 20 min and then the supernatant was collected.

0.4 mmol of HAuCl4 was added to the mixture of CQDs solution (3.25 mg/mL, 30 mL) and MXene (30 mg/mL, 1 mL) solution. Then, the obtained solution reacted in a microwave oven (800 W) at the temperature of 80 °C for 20 min to prepare Au@CQDs-MXene. After freeze-drying, the Au@CQDs-MXene powder was obtained.

Au@CQDs and Au-MXene were prepared by the similar procedure. On one hand, 0.4 mmol of HAuCl4 was added to the CQDs solution (3.25 mg/mL, 30 mL). On the other hand, 0.4 mmol of HAuCl4 was added to MXene (30 mg/mL, 1 mL) solution with 30 mL of deionized water. Then, these two obtained solutions were reacted in a microwave oven (800 W) at the temperature of 80 °C for 20 min to prepare Au@CQDs and Au-MXene.

A glassy carbon electrode (GCE) with a reaction area of 28.27 mm2 was polished with alumina powder several times until mirrored surface appeared. The electrode was sonicated with ethanol and deionized water, then it was dried in a purified nitrogen stream. After dispersing 0.5 mg of Au@CQDs-MXene powder in 0.5 mL of deionized water, 4 μL of Nafion was added to the above system and stirred to get a homogeneous suspension. Finally, 10 μL of the above suspension was dropped to the surface of freshly polished GCE and dried in air at room temperature. GCE decorated with MXene and GCE decorated with CQDs-MXene were fabricated through similar procedures.

The reaction mechanism on the electrode surface can be concluded as the conversion from NO2 – to NO3 – by the following Eq.(1) [27].
(1)
NO
2
-
+
H 2
O →
NO
3
-
+ 2
e
-
+ 2
H
+

The oxidation of nitrite will be accelerated by the nanocomposite when they touch the surface of the electrode. Through this conductive nanocomposite, electron of redox reaction will be captured by the electrode and formed oxidation current, which was presented in the DPV response.

142.1 g of salted vegetables were chopped. Then 400 mL of deionized water was added and pureed in a liquidizer for 2 min. After filtration, the salted vegetables juice was gained, which was added together with nitrite to detecting system as spiked real sample.

All electrochemical measurements were carried out on the CHI660E electrochemical workstation (Shanghai CH Instrument Company, China) in cylindrical electrolytic cells by a traditional three-electrode system. The modified electrode or bare GCE was used as a working electrode. An Ag/AgCl/3M KCl was used as a reference electrode. A platinum electrode was used as a counter electrode. All electrochemical tests were taken at room temperature. Transmission electron microscopes (TEM) images were got from JEM-2100 (JEOL, Japan). Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) images were obtained from scanning electron microscopy (Zeiss, Germany). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) data were got by D8 Advance X-ray Photoelectron Spectroscopy (Bruker, Germany) and were deconvoluted by XPSpeak41. The UV–vis absorption spectrum was investigated by a UV-1800 spectrophotometer (Daojin, China). FT-IR spectra were investigated on a Tensor 27 infrared spectrometer (Bruker, Germany). X-ray diffraction was conducted on a D8 advance X-ray diffractometer (Bruker, Germany).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of CQDs, MXene, Au@CQDs, Au@CQDs-MXene
ce:section.ce:para: The UV–visible absorption spectra of as-synthesized CQDs, MXene, Au@CQDs, Au@CQDs-MXene were recorded by UV–visible absorption spectroscopy (Fig. 1 a). As shown in the UV–vis spectra of CQDs, the two strong characteristic peaks at 230 nm and 325 nm are ascribed to π-π* transition [28]. The spectrum of MXene shows two strong characteristic peaks at 246 nm and 325 nm according to the low bandgap of MXene’s band structure. The UV absorption peak of CQDs near 230 nm is still in the spectrum of Au@CQDs, and it slightly deviates to 229 nm. Although the peak near 325 nm of CQDs becomes very weak and deviates slightly, it is still there. A new UV absorption peak belonging to Au shows at 541 nm. In order to prove that it is CQDs reduce HAuCl4 to AuNPs rather than MXene, Au@CQDs and Au-MXene were prepared. In the spectrum of Au-MXene (Fig.S1), it is a very weak and wide peak centered at about 600 nm, indicating the prepared AuNPs were rare and its size was large and nonuniform. It reveals that the reducibility of MXene is too weak to synthesize AuNPs with high quality. Compared with the spectrum of Au-MXene, that of Au@CQDs presents a strong and narrow characteristic peak at 541 nm, indicating that a great quantity of homogeneous AuNPs were prepared by CQDs. Then, the spectrum of Au@CQDs-MXene shows the characteristic peak near 230 nm attributed to CQDs, the characteristic peak near 541 nm attributed to AuNPs and the characteristic peak near 265 nm ascribed to MXene and CQDs.

The XRD analysis (Fig. 1b) was carried out to confirm the phase structure and crystallinity of MAX, MXene, CQDs, CQDs-MXene and Au@CQDs-MXene nanocomposite. The XRD pattern of MAX (Ti3AlC2) shows a series of peaks at 9.64° and 38.76° corresponded to facets (002) and (104). And the one at 38.76° belongs to Al. However, in the XRD pattern of MXene, the Al peak that belongs to MAX disappears, implying that the Al in MAX was selectively etched by LiF/HCl. In the meantime, the peak of the diffraction of MXene corresponded to facet (002) shifted from 9.64° to 7.07°, testifying to the substantially increasing spacing of layers and the appearance of MXene. The distance of two adjacent layers of MXene was calculated by Bragg’s equation (nλ = 2d·sinθ) [29]. In this equation, the value of n and λ were determined as 1 and 0.15418 nm, respectively. The peak of the diffraction of MXene corresponded to facet (002) was at 7.07°. So, d can be easily calculated from Bragg’s equation as 1.25 nm. At 23.43°, a wide and weak peak belonged to CQDs can be observed, which indicates the highly disordered carbonaceous structure of CQDs [28]. The diffraction of CQDs-MXene shows the characteristic peaks of MXene and CQDs at 6.49° and 25.20°, respectively. The characteristic peak of MXene slightly shifted to the left in the pattern of CQDs-MXene, which means the increasing spacing of MXene layers for loading CQDs. The characteristic peak of MXene continued shifting to the left in the pattern of Au@CQDs-MXene, which implies the continuously increasing spacing of layers for loading bigger Au@CQDs nanoparticles. Except for the characteristic peaks of CQDs and MXene, a series of peaks at 39.09°, 41.77°, 63.83°, 79.71° and 82.39° in the pattern of Au@CQDs-MXene are shown as expected which are corresponded respectively to facets (111), (200), (220), (311) and (222) of Au. It demonstrated the formation of crystalline AuNPs since those peaks generally matched with the standard Au XRD pattern with JCPDS file no. 00–004-0784 [10]. The analytical results above affirm the successful formation of Au@CQDs-MXene nanocomposite.

FT-IR spectra are investigated to identify functional groups and interactions among Au, CQDs and MXene. As shown in Fig. 1c, there are peaks at 3442 cm−1, 1622 cm−1 and 598 cm−1 in the spectrum of MAX, ascribed to OH stretching vibration, carboxyl groups, and Ti-O, respectively. Compared with MAX, these peaks of MXene become stronger for the etching of LiF/HCl [30]. The broad stretching band OH at 3442 cm−1 is due to the OH group on MXene [17]. In the spectrum of CQDs, the strong peaks at 1635 cm−1,1358 cm−1,1000 cm−1 are ascribed to CO stretching vibration, OH bending vibration, CO stretching vibration, respectively. And peaks at 3448 cm−1 and 703 cm−1 are responding to OH stretching vibration. After combining CQDs and MXene to synthesize CQDs-MXene, the OH stretching vibration shifted from 3448 cm−1 (CQDs) and 3450 cm−1 (MXene) to 3446 cm−1 (CQDs-MXene) for the hydrogen bonding interactions. Compared with the spectrum of CQDs, the OH stretching vibration of Au@CQDs shifted from 3448 cm−1 to 3431 cm−1, indicating there was interaction between Au and CQDs. Meanwhile, the CO stretching vibration peak at 1635 cm−1, the OH bending vibration peak at 1358 cm−1 and the CO stretching vibration peak at 1000 cm−1 shifted to 1589 cm−1, 1396 cm−1 and 1078 cm−1, respectively. All these results revealed that CQDs reduced HAuCl4 to AuNPs and combined with AuNPs. As shown in the spectrum of Au@CQDs-MXene, the peak of OH stretching vibration deviates to 3452 cm−1, indicating that extra oxygen-containing groups were generated for the oxidation of CQDs in the preparation of AuNPs. The peaks at 1635 cm−1 and 545 cm−1 confirm the existence of MXene in Au@CQDs-MXene. All these results reveal the successful preparation of Au@CQDs-MXene, which shows the interaction of hydrogen bonding.

To obtain further insight into the componential and structural change of the samples, an X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analysis was performed. As shown in Fig. 1d, Au@CQDs-MXene contains Au, C, N, Ti, O and F. Six typical peaks assigned to Au4f, C1s, N1s, Ti2p, O1s and F1s are located at 84.08 eV, 286.08 eV, 400.08 eV, 456.08 eV, 533.08 eV and 688.08 eV, respectively. Fig. 1e-f and Fig.S2 are a set of high-resolution XPS spectra (Au 4f, Ti 2p, N 1 s) taken from Au@CQDs-MXene to interpret and identify the successful synthesis and chemical structure of Au@CQDs-MXene nanocomposite. Fig. 1e illustrates two distinct peaks of Au4f7/2 and Au4f5/2 at 83.9 eV and 87.6 eV. The difference between these two peaks is 3.7 eV, which identifies the existence of AuNPs [31,32]{Wang, 2016 #39;Niu, 2020 #106}. In addition, no peak from Au3+ can be observed in the figure, which indicates the full reduction of Au3+ to Au atom in the fabrication process [33]. As shown in Fig. 1f, the binding energies of Ti-C 2p3/2, Ti-C 2p1/2, Ti-C Fx are 454.9 eV, 461.2 eV, 463.4 eV, respectively. As for Ti(Ⅱ), the binding energies of Ti(Ⅱ) 2p3/2 and Ti(Ⅱ) 2p1/2 are 455.7 eV and 459.2 eV, respectively. Additionally, the binding energy of Ti (III) is 457.5 eV, and the binding energy of Ti-O is 466.2 eV. The spectrum of Ti 2p in Fig. 1f shows the existence of MXene in the as-fabricated Au@CQDs-MXene [34]. As shown in Fig.S1, the binding energies of pyrrolic N, quaternary N and Graphitic N are 399.2 eV, 400.7 eV and 402.4 eV, respectively. It reveals the existence of CQDs because there is no N element in AuNPs and MXene.

To confirm the morphologies of Au@CQDs-MXene, SEM, TEM, HRTEM and EDS are performed. These images are shown in Fig. 2 -Fig. 3 . MAX is a massive substance in Fig.S3, while the obtained MXene presents very thin lamellar shapes in Fig. 2a after etching. In Fig. 2a, the thickness of the sheet material is about 187.5 nm. However, The distance of two adjacent layers of MXene was calculated from Bragg’s equation as 1.25 nm. It means that hundreds of layers of MXene are stacked together when the intercalation of Au@CQDs are absent. And the spacing of MXene layers is too small to be distinguished by the SEM camera. But fortunately, it can be observed from the XRD pattern of MXene. What’s more, the peak intensity of MXene is evidently lower than that of MAX, which confirms the effective etching of LiF/HCl on MAX. The thin lamellar MXene sheets can be further certified in Fig. 2d. A lot of aggregative granules are shown in Fig. 2b. As shown in Fig. 2e, these spherical granules with diameter about 20 nm are AuNPs, which are reduced by CQDs (0.3–0.5 nm). Their structures were further characterized by HRTEM. HRTEM images of Au@CQDs in the inset of Fig. 3d-f reveal a lattice spacing of 0.233 nm which is in coherence with the (111) plane of Au [35]. After the treatment of the green one-pot synthesis method, AuNPs and CQDs distributed uniformly on the surface of MXene (Fig. 2c). Furthermore, the well uniform distribution of Au@CQDs on MXene is found in Fig. 2f and Fig. 3a.

EDS is carried out to demonstrate the element composition and distribution of Au@CQDs-MXene in Fig. 3b-f. Fig. 3b shows the general situation of the Au@CQDs-MXene. The distributions of Ti and O in Fig. 3c and Fig. 3e denote the edge of the sample, but a few O elements can be seen outside the edge of the sample in the inset (Fig. 3b and Fig. 3e). This is owing to the existence of O elements in the carbon network which consists of the background. Located on the surface of MXene, CQDs are represented by the N element in the image (Fig. 3d). N element is in a similar situation with the O element, in which a few N element also present outside the edge of the sample. Then the uniform distribution of AuNPs on the surface of MXene sheets is indicated by Fig. 3f. These results agree well with the XRD analysis and the FT-IR analysis.

All these results confirmed that Au@CQDs-MXene nanocomposite was successfully prepared. In this nanocomposite, CQDs play an important role as the green in situ reducing agent, which ensures the reliable reduction of AuNPs. Au@CQDs were attached uniformly to the surface of MXene to prevent the agglomeration of MXene, while MXene acts as an immobilized matrix to disperse Au@CQDs. Thus, Au@CQDs-MXene is expected to have good electrochemical properties.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrochemical behaviors of GCE and modified GCEs
ce:section.ce:para: The currents resulting from the nitrite oxidation reaction are captured by the electrodes. Then the electrochemical behaviors of different electrodes have been investigated by cyclic voltammetry. As shown in Fig. 4 a, the cyclic voltammograms of bare GCE, MXene, CQDs, Au@CQDs, CQDs-MXene, Au@CQDs-MXene were investigated in 0.1 M PBS (pH 7.0) solution with 800 μM NO2 –. As a result, although bare GCE shows a weak anodic peak, the electrocatalytic ability of bare GCE is too weak to catalyze nitrite oxidation reaction efficiently. Casting MXene on GCE helps amplify the oxidation current, which is an outstanding current collector with metal conductivity and a large specific surface area. Owing to the oxygen-containing functional groups on the surface of MXene proved in FT-IR spectra, the electrochemical catalysis of MXene/GCE becomes poor. So the onset potential of MXene is larger than that of bare GCE [36]. Casting CQDs on GCE made a great difference in the onset potential of the electrode. It reveals that CQDs effectively catalyzed the oxidation of nitrite. It is noted that GCE with CQDs-MXene shows not only a higher anodic peak but also a smaller onset potential than bare GCE and GCE with MXene, which affirms the positive effect on nitrite catalysis brought by CQDs. What’s more, the results of MXene/GCE, CQDs-MXene/GCE and Au@CQDs/GCE indicate that CQDs, MXene and AuNPs are all capable to promote the oxidation of nitrite at the electrode. Then GCE with Au@CQDs-MXene shows the highest oxidation peak current implying the highest sensitivity for nitrite [36], and the smallest onset potential implying the easiest oxidation reaction on nitrite [37]. The significant increase of the oxidation peak indicates the fast electron transfer rate at the electrode. And this increase is evidence of the high electrocatalysis gained from the synergy of the CQDs, MXene and AuNPs. Furthermore, compared with CQDs-MXene, Au@CQDs-MXene presents a remarkable optimization of oxidation current and onset potential. It indicates the excellent conductivity of AuNPs also helps a lot in overcoming the negative influence of those oxygen-containing functional groups on MXene. The synergism of Au, CQDs and MXene show harmonious cooperation of nitrite oxidation, electron gathering and amplifying.

To investigate the interfacial properties between the electrode surface and electrolyte solutions, the electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) of different modified GCEs were performed in a solution containing 800 μM NO2 –, 0.1 M PBS and 3 M KCl at a frequency range of 1–100 kHz. In Fig. 4b, the Nyquist curves show the linear part of modified electrodes within low frequencies, which is dominated by a diffusion-limited process [18]. The Nyquist curve can be fitted according to the equivalent circuit in the illustration in Fig. 4b. The electron transfer resistance (Rct) value of bare GCE, MXene/GCE CQDs-MXene/GCE, Au@CQDs/GCE and Au@CQDs-MXene/GCE were calculated to be 12204 Ω, 59.43 Ω, 9261 Ω, 934 Ω and 185.2 Ω, respectively. The Rct of bare GCE is the largest, and casting materials on the surface of the electrode improved the conductivity. CQDs showed outstanding catalytic activity on the oxidation of nitrite, but its poor conductivity needs to be improved. MXene/GCE showed the lowest Rct, indicating the distinct performance to boost electron transportation. Furthermore, AuNPs could contribute a lot to decrease the Rct of the modified electrode, compared with the fitted results of CQDs-MXene/GCE and Au@CQDs-MXene/GCE. Therefore, the fabricated Au@CQDs-MXene/GCE by combining CQDs, AuNPs and MXene was harmonious with low resistance and high electrocatalytic activity.

Aimed at investigating the kinetics of electrode reaction, CV was carried out to investigate the effect of scan rate on the electrical catalytic oxidation of nitrite in 0.1 M PBS (pH 7.0). As shown in Fig. 4c, with the increase of scan rate, the intensity of anodic currents peak increases. The small diagram in Fig. 4c shows a good linear relationship between oxidation current and the square root of scan rates with the linear regression values (R2) of 0.998. It denotes that the electrochemical oxidation of nitrite is dominated by a diffusion-controlled process [37]. Furthermore, pH shows a markable influence on the anodic peak currents. As shown in Fig. 4d, when the pH value is 5.5, the anodic peak current increases with the increase of pH until achieving maximum at pH 7.0. This is because that NO2 – is not stable and easily oxidized into NO3 – in the solution below pH 7.0 [38]. When the pH value increases beyond 7.0, the anodic peak current decreases for the lack of protons, which hinders the oxidation process of nitrite [16,38]. In conclusion, pH 7.0 is the best condition for the sensitive detection of nitrite with Au@CQDs-MXene/GCE.

DPV was utilized for investigating the relationship between the currents and the concentrations of nitrite. As displayed in Fig. 5 a-b, the peak current increases with the increasing concentration, which indicates the effective modification of the electrode for nitrite catalytic oxidation. It is observable that the linear relationship between current and concentration is divided into two linear ranges. The red calibration curve shows the first detection scope from 1 μM to 500 μM, and the linear regression equation is I1 = 0.04037C + 3.993 with an R2 of 0.999. The blue calibration curve shows the other detection scope from 500 μM to 3200 μM, the linear regression equation is I2 = 0.03C + 8.967 with an R2 of 0.998. Hence, the detection limit (S/N = 3) was estimated to be 0.078 μM. The good linear regression equations and the fast responses toward nitrite verify the ability of Au@CQDs-MXene modified GCE on nitrite detection.

Besides the superior electric response, selectivity is crucial to an ideal nitrite detection sensing platform in practical applications, so some coexisting inorganic interferences were added to evaluate the selectivity of Au@CQDs-MXene/GCE. As presented in Fig. 5c, a sharp current response appears rapidly when NaNO2 was added. However, the current responses are almost negligible when interfering compounds such as NaCl, NaSO4, KNO3, CaCl2, MgCl2, NaAc and CuCl2 were added. When NaNO2 with the same concentration was added again, the sensing platform showed a rapid and same amount of current response. So, the Au@CQDs-MXene/GCE has the ability of accurately detect nitrite under the circumstances of coexistence of multiple heteroions.

By exposing Au@CQDs-MXene/GCE in the air at room temperature for 14 days, the stability of Au@CQDs-MXene/GCE was investigated by CV and the results are displayed in Fig. 5d. After 14 days, the potential retained 98.65% of its initial value, and the current retained 101.71% of its initial value. After long-time storage of 2 weeks, the current and potential are almost steady-state, indicating the preeminent stability of Au@CQDs-MXene/GCE.

Au@CQDs-Mxene/GCE was applied to detect nitrite in actual experiments using real samples of tap water and vegetables to further illustrate the practical application of the developed sensing platform through standard addition methods. As listed in Table 1 , recoveries of the three measurements are 103.6%, 98.1% and 101.1%. Meanwhile, the RSD is 4.2%, 2.5% and 1.2% (n = 3). These satisfactory recoveries provide adequate evidence that the Au@CQDs-Mxene/GCE sensor has bright prospects for nitrite detection in tap water. Au@CQDs-Mxene/GCE was also applied to detect nitrite in salted vegetables. As listed in Table 2 , recoveries of the three measurements are 101.3%, 93.2% and 100.8%. Meanwhile, the RSD is 5.2%, 4.3% and 10.9% (n = 3). These results prove that the Au@CQDs-MXene/GCE sensor was adaptable for nitrite detection in salted vegetables.

The detecting performance of Au@CQDs-MXene/GCE is compared with other reported nitrite sensing platforms and the results are listed in Table 3 . Compared with other reported nitrite sensing platforms, these results further confirm low detection limit and relatively wide linear range of the Au@CQDs-MXene/GCE nitrite sensing platform using differential pulse voltammetry (DPV) method. The prepared AuNPs@CQDs-MXene combined the outstanding electrocatalytic performance of AuNPs with CQDs and the excellent conductivity of AuNPs with MXene. The MXene acted as current nano collector, which amplified the oxidation current in the detection of nitrite. Compared with graphene-AuNP and AuNPs/MWCPE, in this work, AuNPs were stabilized by CQDs and their electrochemical properties had been made full used. So, AuNPs@CQDs-MXene showed a wider detection range and lower LOD. Therefore, the Au@CQDs-MXene/GCE electrode can be considered as a novel and effective electrochemical sensing platform for nitrite.

In summary, we testified a simple one-pot synthesis of Au@CQDs-MXene nanocomposite using CQDs as the green in situ reducing agent. Our prepared AuNPs@CQDs-MXene combined the outstanding electrocatalytic performance of AuNPs with CQDs and the excellent electrical conductivity of AuNPs with MXene. Compared to other sensors like graphene-AuNP and AuNPs/MWCPE, AuNPs were stabilized by CQDs and their electrochemical properties had been combined in this work. Therefore, when Au@CQDs-MXene nanocomposite was loaded on GCE to construct a sensing platform for nitrite detection, this sensing platform possessed various advantages such as a wide linear range for detection, low detection limit, high stability, and good reproducibility, Importantly, those outstanding properties make it viable to detect nitrite in tap water and salted vegetables, which can get good recoveries. So the Au@CQDs-MXene/GCE sensor shows a new pathway to the fabrication and application of nitrite sensors. And the lifetime of the modified electrode sensor will be further improved in the future work.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the Guangdong Basic and Applied Basic Research Foundation (No. 2020B1515120038), the State Key Laboratory of Pulp and Paper Engineering (No. 2020ZR05), and the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities (No. 2020ZYGXZR066).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jcis.2021.09.036.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
